title: LLM-BabyBench: Understanding and Evaluating Grounded Planning and Reasoning in LLMs
authors: Omar Choukrani, Idriss Malek, Daniil Orel, Zhuohan Xie, Zangir Iklassov, Martin Takáč, Salem Lahlou
venue: arXiv preprint 2505.12135, v1, submitted 17 May 2025. No published venue listed on the arXiv abstract page (preprint only) as of the local fetch (2026-07-22).
claim: LLM-BabyBench is a benchmark suite built on a textual adaptation of the procedurally generated BabyAI grid world, evaluating LLMs on three fundamental grounded-reasoning tasks: predicting the consequences of an action sequence on environment state (Predict), generating low-level action sequences to reach a subgoal (Plan), and decomposing a high-level instruction into a subgoal sequence (Decompose). The authors report that current state-of-the-art LLMs (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o, Llama 3.1 family, DeepSeek-R1-Distill, Qwen3-32B) show clear, non-saturated performance gaps and distinct failure modes across all three tasks, arguing the benchmark isolates grounded spatial/planning reasoning from perceptual and 3D-simulation confounds present in prior benchmarks (ALFRED/ALFWorld/AI2-THOR).
novelty: Authors claim novelty on three fronts vs prior grounded-agent benchmarks (AI2-THOR, Habitat, TextWorld, Jericho, ScienceWorld, MineDojo, ALFRED, ALFWorld, GameTraversalBenchmark): (1) a fully controlled, purely textual, symbolic-state interface over BabyAI/MiniGrid that isolates reasoning from 3D-vision/perception confounds; (2) a structured-data-extraction methodology that decomposes expert-bot trajectories into three complementary reasoning-diagnostic tasks (state prediction, low-level planning, hierarchical subgoal decomposition) rather than just logging raw trajectories for imitation learning; (3) an 'omniscient perspective' setup (full observability, no partial-observability constraint) paired with a custom 'BabyAI OmniBot' oracle (100% solve rate) that serves as an upper-bound/ground-truth generator, plus a standardized open-source evaluation harness with environment-executed verification for Plan/Decompose (Sec. 1, Sec. 3, Table 1, Sec. 4.3).

protocol.description: For the Predict task (the c19-relevant split), the model receives one prompt containing (i) the full textual environment description (all objects/positions revealed regardless of agent position, 'Omniscient Perspective'), (ii) the agent's initial position and orientation, and (iii) an ordered action sequence (e.g., left, forward, pick-up). The model outputs its prediction of the final state in a single forward pass; 'No interaction with the environment is allowed at inference time; the model must rely entirely on its internal representation of state dynamics' (Sec. 4.4.1). This is single-shot/full-observation, matching c19's format. Separately, the Plan task is evaluated via environment execution (interactive, ground-truth verified by running the LLM-generated action sequence in the simulator, Sec. 4.4.2), and Decompose is evaluated by having the OmniBot execute the LLM-generated subgoal sequence (Sec. 4.4.3, Appendix D.4) -- so the paper as a whole spans both single-shot (Predict) and simulator-executed/interactive-verification (Plan, Decompose) protocols, though the LLM itself never receives step-by-step environment feedback in any of the three tasks; only Predict is single-call end-to-end.
protocol.observation_content: Full environment state at once, in one of three text formats compared in Appendix B.5/B.5.1: (1) Narrative -- natural English paragraph describing room/grid dimensions, coordinate convention, all actions, then an enumerated list of every object with color/type/position embedded in prose, then the agent's initial position/direction and mission string; (2) Structured -- same context sentence followed by bulleted {key:value} fields (Number of rooms, Room size, Grid size, Agent initial position, Agent facing direction, Objects in environment as a bulleted list of type/color/position/locked, Mission); (3) JSON -- a 'context' string plus a 'config' JSON object with fields num_rooms, room_size_incl_walls, room_size_excl_walls, grid_size, agent_initial_pos, agent_front_pos, agent_direction (index+name), objects (list of dicts with type/color/position/locked), mission. All three formats give the FULL grid content (every object's exact coordinate), not an ASCII-rendered grid image/matrix -- there is no literal grid-of-characters rendering shown in the extraction; the environment is described via coordinate-labeled object lists plus a prose/structured/JSON header, not a printed ASCII map (Appendix B.5 verbatim examples, Sec. 4.2). The model is also given the action sequence to simulate and must output the final agent position/direction (and any state changes from pickup/drop/toggle) -- there is no distance-to-wall or per-step feedback since it is single-shot.

representations.formats_compared: Three formatting strategies for the SAME underlying full-observability content: Narrative (natural English sentences), Structured (sentence context + bulleted {key:value} pairs), JSON (sentence context + JSON blob) -- Sec. 4.2, examples in Appendix B.5.
representations.effect_on_accuracy: Table 6 (Appendix B.5.1, averaged over all models, Predict task, ToT prompting fixed): Narrative = 56.78% success rate / 2.34 Manhattan distance; JSON = 59.28% / 2.65; Structured text = 61.08% / 2.24. Structured format wins on both metrics and is adopted for the published dataset and Sec. 5 evaluations ('Given the superiority of the Structured format ... we use this formatter in the published dataset and in our evaluations of Section 5', Sec. 4.2).
representations.note: No ASCII-grid-rendering vs coordinate-list vs adjacency-list comparison is reported; the paper's three formats are all text-based full-object-listing variants (prose vs bullets vs JSON), not visual/matrix vs symbolic-list comparisons. This is a narrower representation-comparison than what c19's dossier ask implies (e.g. no 'visual/matrix' arm) -- marked here as the extraction's actual scope, not a gap.

models.name: Claude 3.7 Sonnet
models.strategies: Zero-shot, few-shot (3 examples from OmniBot optimal solutions), CoT, ToT (Tree-of-Thought) all compared in Appendix C; ToT selected as the strategy for all Sec. 5 results because it 'consistently outperformed the others' (Appendix C, Table 7).
models.key_results: Predict Success Rate by difficulty (Table 3): Easy 97.50%, Moderate 94.29%, Hard 86.67%, Very Hard 82.50% -- strongest model overall, '80% of samples' correct on hardest levels (Sec. 5.2.1). Table 4 competency breakdown: Room Navigation 98.33%, Maze Navigation 85.50%, Sequences of Commands 80.00% (only model not degrading sharply on sequences). Plan task: best on Ultra grids (~20% success, Sec. 5.2.2/Fig. 1). Decompose: one of only two models (with Qwen) achieving non-zero Precision Rate at 7-9 Subgoals-Added (Sec. 5.2.3).

models.name: GPT-4o
models.strategies: Same zero-shot/few-shot/CoT/ToT comparison; ToT used in Sec. 5 (Appendix C, Sec. 5.1).
models.key_results: Predict (Table 3): Easy 97.50%, Moderate 84.68%, Hard 76.80%, Very Hard 61.19%. Table 4: Room Navigation 93.98%, Maze Navigation 69.90%, Sequences of Commands 53.52%. Plan: failed to exceed 30% success rate even on small grids (Sec. 5.2.2).

models.name: DeepSeek-R1-Distill (Llama-3.1-70B distilled from DeepSeek-R1)
models.strategies: ToT (Sec. 5.1); reasoning-model behavior noted qualitatively in Appendix F.
models.key_results: Predict (Table 3): Easy 97.50%, Moderate 68.61%, Hard 50.79%, Very Hard 37.24% -- steepest degradation with difficulty among top models. Table 4: Maze Navigation only 44.21% vs 89.08% Room Navigation, Sequences of Commands 31.14%. Plan: one of the strongest 'reasoning models' (~80% on small grids per Sec. 5.2.2, Fig. 1) alongside Qwen3. Failure trace (Appendix F.1.2, BossLevel seed 47): fails where Claude succeeds; qualitatively 'starts deviating from the main task, sometimes even questioning the exactitude of the description of the grid given in the prompt' (Sec. 5.2.1).

models.name: Meta-Llama-3.1-405B
models.key_results: Predict (Table 3): Easy 85.00%, Moderate 53.26%, Hard 62.19% (non-monotonic), Very Hard 41.25%.

models.name: Meta-Llama-3.1-70B
models.key_results: Predict (Table 3): Easy 63.82%, Moderate 39.36%, Hard 32.54%, Very Hard 33.95%.

models.name: Meta-Llama-3.1-8B
models.key_results: Predict (Table 3): Easy 12.89%, Moderate 4.47%, Hard 2.08%, Very Hard 2.78% -- weakest model, near-floor. Table 4 Sequences of Commands: 0.00%.

models.name: Qwen3-32B
models.key_results: Predict (Table 3): Easy 95.00%, Moderate 65.48%, Hard 61.93%, Very Hard 48.82%. Plan: strong reasoning-model performance (~80% on small grids, Fig. 1). Decompose: with Claude, one of only two models with non-zero PR at 7-9 Subgoals-Added (Sec. 5.2.3).

headline_numbers: Predict Success Rate across difficulty, all 7 models (Table 3, Sec. 5.2.1): ranges from Claude's 82.50% (Very Hard) down to Llama-3.1-8B's 2.78% (Very Hard); Easy-level scores are high (85-97.5%) for Claude, DeepSeek-R1-Distill, GPT-4o, Llama-405B, and Qwen3, but Llama-3.1-70B is a notable non-monotonic outlier at only 63.82% on Easy (below even Llama-405B's 85.00%), and Llama-3.1-8B is far lower still (12.89%) -- so Easy-level scores do NOT cluster uniformly near ceiling across all but the 8B model.

Formatting comparison averaged over all models on Predict (Table 6, Appendix B.5.1): Structured text 61.08% success / 2.24 Manhattan distance is best; Narrative 56.78% / 2.34; JSON 59.28% / 2.65 -- a ~4-5 point swing in success rate purely from prompt formatting.

Prompting-strategy comparison on Predict (Table 7, Appendix C, averaged over all models, GoToObj + BossLevel missions, 20 seeds): ToT 62.62% success / 2.41 L1 > CoT 61.07% / 2.79 > Zero-Shot 59.29% / 3.09 > Few-Shot 59.05% / 2.47 -- ToT adopted for all Sec. 5 results.
OmniBot (expert symbolic oracle) achieves 100% solve rate across all 16 benchmark levels, serving as the upper bound / ground-truth generator (Sec. 4.3).

Plan task grid-size scaling (Sec. 5.2.2, Fig. 1): small (8x8, up to 7 obstacles), medium (16x16, up to 60), large (24x24, up to 120), ultra (32x32, up to 180); reasoning models (Qwen3, DeepSeek-R1-Distill) reach ~80% success on small grids but none of them beats 50% on medium/large; on ultra grids 'only the reasoning models are capable of scoring over 0' (Sec. 5.2.2) -- i.e. more than one model scores above zero on Ultra, with Claude-3.7 reaching the highest value (~20% max), not Claude alone scoring above 0.
Decompose Precision Rate collapses with mission complexity (Sec. 5.2.3): non-zero PR only for Qwen and Claude at 7-9 Subgoals-Added (SA); 'for 10+ SA, all models have PR=0'.
failure_modes: State drift / cumulative position error: Manhattan-distance metric (Table 2, Table 3/6, Appendix D.2) is specifically designed to quantify degree of spatial error on incorrect Predict answers, implying incorrect predictions are graded, not binary-random misses -- consistent with accumulating small positional errors over an action sequence rather than catastrophic derailment.
Difficulty-correlated degradation: 'their accuracy drops significantly as difficulty increases' for GPT-4o, DeepSeek-R1-Distilled, and Qwen3, while Claude holds up much better (Sec. 5.2.1).

Maze/multi-room navigation is disproportionately harder than single-room navigation for most models: 'models such as DeepSeek-R1-Distilled, GPT-4o, and Qwen3 perform well in single-room environments, their accuracy drops substantially in maze-like scenarios' (Sec. 5.2.1, Table 4: e.g. DeepSeek-R1-Distill Room Navigation 89.08% vs Maze Navigation 44.21%).

Sequences-of-instructions failure: 'with the exception of Claude, all models exhibit significant difficulties when processing sequences of instructions, which often require longer navigation paths and interaction with multiple objects' (Sec. 5.2.1; Table 4 Sequences of Commands column drops sharply for every non-Claude model, e.g. Llama-3.1-8B = 0.00%).

Qualitative reasoning-trace failure (Appendix F, BossLevel seed 47): DeepSeek-R1-Distill 'starts deviating from the main task, sometimes even questioning the exactitude of the description of the grid given in the prompt,' whereas Claude 'solely focus[es] on calculating the next state at each step as a human would do' (Sec. 5.2.1).

Plan-task scaling failure with grid size, not obstacle count: 'the main factor determining the failure of the models on our Plan task is the size of the grid. Within the same size the number of obstacles does not affect the success rate' (Appendix E.2).

Decompose long-horizon failure: 'current LLMs cannot effectively decompose long-horizon missions into elementary subgoals while accounting for the agent's operational environment' -- PR reaches 0 for all models at 10+ Subgoals-Added (Sec. 5.2.3).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The Predict task is explicitly designed as a strict one-forward-prediction test: the model sees the full initial state plus the entire action sequence in one prompt and must output the final state with 'No interaction with the environment ... allowed at inference time; the model must rely entirely on its internal representation of state dynamics' (Sec. 4.4.1). This isolates 'whether the language model has learned a sufficiently detailed world model to reason about transitions deterministically' (Sec. 4.4.1) -- i.e., single-shot full simulation of a whole trajectory, not the step-by-step per-action prediction c19 also avoids. Contrast: the Plan and Decompose tasks are NOT single-shot from the environment's perspective -- the LLM still produces its full output in one call (no env feedback during generation), but correctness is checked by executing that output across an interactive loop in the real simulator (env executes the LLM's action sequence for Plan, Sec. 4.4.2; the OmniBot executes the LLM's subgoal sequence for Decompose, appending its own subgoals as needed, Sec. 4.4.3/Appendix D.4). The paper's own conclusion is that models are comparatively much stronger at single-shot Predict on short/easy sequences (Easy-level Success Rate 85-97.5% for five of the seven models, Table 3 -- though Llama-3.1-70B is a 63.82% outlier at Easy, below even the larger 405B variant) than at multi-step generation validated by interaction (Plan tops out well under 50% success on medium/large grids for all models, and even the best model only reaches ~20% on ultra grids, Sec. 5.2.2) -- i.e., one-shot full-state prediction is comparatively tractable at low difficulty but degrades with action-sequence length/difficulty, while interactively-verified multi-step plan generation is harder across the board and scales worse with environment size than with reasoning depth alone.

relation_to_candidate.quadrant: Full-observation x Single-shot (for the Predict split specifically) -- this is the SAME quadrant as c19. The paper's Predict task and c19 are both: (a) full-observation (LLM-BabyBench's 'Omniscient Perspective' reveals all objects regardless of agent position, Sec. 4.2/4.3, matching c19's full ASCII-grid-shown-at-once design), and (b) single-shot (one prompt containing initial state + full action/command sequence, one predicted output, no environment interaction allowed at inference, Sec. 4.4.1, matching c19's 'shown all at once... predicts ONE derived fact' design). Note the paper's OTHER two tasks (Plan, Decompose) sit in a different cell: full-observation but interactively-verified-via-simulator-execution rather than single-shot-scored.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c19: Very high structural overlap on the Predict split: both give the model (i) a full/omniscient rendering of a grid environment, (ii) an initial agent pose, (iii) an ordered command/action sequence, and ask for (iv) a single derived fact about the final state (c19: coordinate/heading/carrying-flag; LLM-BabyBench Predict: final agent position + direction, scored by exact match plus Manhattan-distance partial credit). Both use seeded/procedural generation for contamination resistance (LLM-BabyBench via BabyAI's procedural level generator + seeds, Sec. 4.1; c19 via its own seeded ASCII generator). Both compare prompt/representation formatting effects on accuracy (LLM-BabyBench: Narrative/Structured/JSON, Table 6; c19: convention strata across turn/wrap/indexing rules).
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Observation format: LLM-BabyBench never renders a literal ASCII grid map -- state is always a prose/bulleted/JSON list of labeled object coordinates plus agent pose, not a printed character grid (Appendix B.5); c19 explicitly shows an ASCII grid rendering. (2) Action vocabulary: LLM-BabyBench uses BabyAI's fixed 6 actions (left, right, forward, pickup, drop, toggle) with standard semantics; c19 adds invented/nonstandard commands (e.g., jump-two, wrap) specifically to defeat memorized conventions -- LLM-BabyBench has no analogous invented-rule stratum. (3) Edge/wall handling: LLM-BabyBench's grid is a multi-room BabyAI maze with walls/doors/keys as game objects, not a wrap-vs-clamp boundary convention; c19's wrap-versus-clamp axis has no direct counterpart in the paper. (4) Scale: LLM-BabyBench grids run from 6x6 single rooms up to 22x22 multi-room mazes (BossLevel, e.g. Appendix B.5 example) -- substantially larger than c19's roughly-6x6 target. (5) Metric granularity: LLM-BabyBench reports both exact-match Success Rate AND Manhattan-distance partial credit for misses (Table 2/3, Appendix D.2), whereas c19 is strict 0/1 exact match only. (6) Best prompting strategy differs: LLM-BabyBench found ToT best (Appendix C); c19's dossier does not specify a required prompting strategy.
relation_to_candidate.what_c19_would_add: c19 would add: (a) an invented-command layer (nonstandard action semantics unknown to any pretrained convention) that LLM-BabyBench's Predict task does not have -- LLM-BabyBench uses only the standard, well-documented BabyAI action set, so a model could in principle have memorized action semantics from BabyAI's own public documentation/code, whereas c19's invented commands cannot be memorized; (b) an explicit wrap-vs-clamp boundary-condition stratum, orthogonal to anything in LLM-BabyBench's wall/door-based obstacle model; (c) a literal ASCII-grid rendering as the observation format, testing whether models parse a spatial character grid directly rather than a coordinate-labeled object list (LLM-BabyBench's Appendix B.5.1 formatting study never tests a rendered grid/matrix arm, only prose/bullets/JSON over the same coordinate-list content); (d) much smaller/cheaper grids (~6x6 vs LLM-BabyBench's up to 22x22), trading ecological richness (multi-room mazes, keys/doors/colored objects) for a tighter, cheaper, more strictly bounded exact-match instrument; (e) a codebase (Minigrid) that is actively maintained and run-verified for seed reproducibility, whereas LLM-BabyBench's own repo/dataset status is not independently verified in this dossier (only cited as 'publicly available' per the paper's own Abstract/Conclusion).

corpus_claim: LLM-BabyBench is a textual BabyAI grid world; its Predict split (predict the final environment state after executing a low-level action sequence) is the c19 template, with ground truth extracted from an expert agent in the simulator.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The core claim holds: LLM-BabyBench IS a textual adaptation of the BabyAI grid world (Sec. 4.1-4.2), its Predict split IS defined exactly as 'predict the final environment state (agent position + direction, plus any pickup/drop/toggle-induced object-state changes) after executing a given low-level action sequence' (Sec. 4.4.1), and ground truth for Predict IS derived from an expert agent -- but precision matters: the ground truth is generated by the 'BabyAI OmniBot' (Sec. 4.3), an ENHANCED/modified version of the original BabyAI expert bot given full grid visibility, not the original partially-observed BabyAI Bot, and technically the Predict target_state is the actual resulting simulator state after executing the given action sequence (a deterministic environment rollout), rather than something the OmniBot must 'solve' from scratch -- the OmniBot/expert-agent machinery in this paper is centrally used for the Plan and Decompose ground truth and for the Predict task's underlying (initial_state, action_sequence) pairs are drawn from expert (OmniBot) trajectories, per Sec. 4.4.1: 'The datasets for this task contain tuples of the form: (level_name, seed, env_description, initial_state, action_sequence, target_state).' So 'extracted from an expert agent in the simulator' is correct in that the action sequences themselves originate from OmniBot demonstrations, but the target_state is a straightforward deterministic environment execution result, not itself a bot 'solve' -- a minor but real precision gap in the corpus claim's phrasing, hence partially-supported rather than fully supported.
baseline_relevant: Predict task inputs use full-observability 'Omniscient Perspective' state descriptions (never a rendered ASCII grid) in one of three text formats; Structured format (sentence context + bulleted key:value pairs) gave the best average Success Rate (61.08%) and lowest Manhattan distance (2.24) vs Narrative (56.78%/2.34) and JSON (59.28%/2.65) -- Table 6, Appendix B.5.1. If our own baseline uses a coordinate-list style representation rather than literal ASCII, structured bullet-point framing outperformed prose and JSON by 2-5 points.

ToT (Tree-of-Thought) prompting outperformed Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, and CoT on Predict (Table 7, Appendix C): 62.62% vs 59.29%/59.05%/61.07% respectively, averaged across all 7 models on GoToObj + BossLevel, 20 seeds -- worth testing multi-path reasoning prompts for our baseline rather than defaulting to plain CoT.

Metrics used: exact-match Success Rate (binary, per-sample) AND Manhattan/L1 distance between predicted and true agent coordinates computed ONLY over incorrect predictions (Table 2, Appendix D.1-D.2) -- a graded partial-credit metric layered on top of strict exact match, useful if we want a diagnostic secondary metric beyond pure 0/1.

Difficulty floors/ceilings observed on Predict (Table 3): high (85-97.5%) Easy-level Success Rate for five of seven models, dropping to Claude 82.50% / Llama-8B 2.78% at Very Hard; note Llama-3.1-70B is a non-monotonic outlier at only 63.82% on Easy (lower than the larger 405B variant's 85.00%), so the Easy-level ceiling is NOT universal across all but the smallest model -- confirms grid-state-prediction floors are NOT uniformly low; the naive-prompt floor risk c19's own dossier flags (round-1 'grid navigation is a familiar skill') is directly visible here: standard BabyAI action semantics alone do not floor most frontier models on Easy levels, reinforcing that c19's invented-command/wrap-vs-clamp strata are necessary to avoid a too-high floor.

Grid/level scale for the Predict task follows BabyAI's native level sizes: base rooms are 6x6 (single-room levels) up to 22x22 multi-room mazes for BossLevel/SynthSeq (Appendix B.5 worked example: 3x3 rooms of 8x8 each, 22x22 total grid) -- much larger than c19's proposed ~6x6 target grid; our baseline can be considerably smaller/cheaper and still occupy the same task family.

Plan-task grid sizes for reference (different task, same paper): small 8x8 (up to 7 obstacles), medium 16x16 (up to 60), large 24x24 (up to 120), ultra 32x32 (up to 180) -- Sec. 5.2.2. Not directly transferable to c19's Predict-style single-fact task, but useful as a scale reference if grid-size strata are considered later.

Data/release status: the paper states datasets, data-generation code, and evaluation code are 'made publicly available (GitHub, HuggingFace)' (Abstract, Conclusion) -- exact repo URLs/DOIs are not present in this local extraction (noted gap; see below). No explicit contamination discussion is given beyond the standard argument that BabyAI's procedural generator + seeds keeps instances reproducible/regenerable.

Model set and inference platform: evaluations used DeepInfra for open-weight inference and native APIs for Claude/GPT-4o (Sec. 5.1) -- useful as a precedent for which providers/access routes were used for a comparable multi-model sweep.
quotes: "The model is then tasked with predicting the final state after executing these actions in order. No interaction with the environment is allowed at inference time; the model must rely entirely on its internal representation of state dynamics." (Sec. 4.4.1)

"In our current benchmark, we provide the LLM with full observability of the environment. The environment descriptions reveal the positions of all objects in the grid regardless of the agent's position or direction." (Sec. 4.3)

"Therefore, we explore three formatting strategies: (1) Narrative format ... (2) Structured format ... and (3) JSON format ... Given the superiority of the Structured format in terms of metrics described in Section 4.5, we use this formatter in the published dataset and in our evaluations of Section 5." (Sec. 4.2)
"Among all models evaluated, Claude demonstrates the strongest performance, correctly predicting both position and direction in 80% of the samples, even on the most challenging levels." (Sec. 5.2.1)

"The OmniBot achieves a 100% solve rate across all levels included in the benchmark. This perfect performance provides a robust standard and an effective upper bound for evaluating LLM performance." (Sec. 4.3)
"This demonstrates that current LLMs cannot effectively decompose long-horizon missions into elementary subgoals while accounting for the agent's operational environment." (Sec. 5.2.3)

corrections: headline_numbers[0] (Predict Success Rate across difficulty): corrected false claim that 'Easy-level scores cluster near ceiling (85-97.5%) for all but the 8B model' -- Table 3 shows Meta-Llama-3.1-70B scores only 63.82% on Easy (below even the larger 405B model's 85.00%), so the Easy-level ceiling does not hold for all but the 8B model; Llama-3.1-70B is also a non-monotonic near-ceiling outlier.

headline_numbers[4] (Plan task grid-size scaling on Ultra grids): corrected false claim that 'only Claude-3.7 scores above 0 on ultra' -- the paper's exact text (Sec. 5.2.2) is 'only the reasoning models are capable of scoring over 0 on them, and the maximum performance is about 20% (Claude-3.7)', meaning multiple reasoning models (not Claude alone) score above zero on Ultra grids, with Claude reaching the highest value (~20%).
one_shot_vs_multistep field: corrected the same 'near ceiling 85-97.5% at Easy' overgeneralization to note the Llama-3.1-70B Easy-difficulty outlier (63.82%), consistent with the Table 3 data.

baseline_relevant entry on Predict difficulty floors/ceilings: corrected the same 'near-ceiling on Easy for all but the smallest model' overgeneralization to flag the Llama-3.1-70B non-monotonic outlier at 63.82% on Easy.

Checked and confirmed as accurate (no changes needed): all Table 3 and Table 4 per-model numbers in the 'models' array (verified column-by-column against source for possible table-linearization misalignment -- none found); Table 6 and Table 7 formatting/prompting numbers; all eight verbatim quotes (located and matched word-for-word in source, including section attribution); protocol_mode classification (Predict is single-shot/full-observation; Plan and Decompose are full-observation but interactively/simulator-verified -- this three-way split is preserved correctly, not flattened); observation_content claim that no literal ASCII grid rendering is ever shown to the model (confirmed against Appendix B.5 narrative/structured/JSON examples and Figures 3-4, which are rendered images, not text-based ASCII art); OmniBot 100% solve rate claim; Decompose SA/PR quotes; corpus_claim_verdict's characterization of target_state as a deterministic environment-execution result versus an OmniBot 'solve'.
